message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Importance</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>While COVID-19 vaccines are highly effective against disease, breakthrough infections may occur in the context of rising variants of concern.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Objective</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>We paired random and passive surveillance nucleic acid testing with analysis of viral whole genomic sequences to detect and describe breakthrough infections, focusing in a university community.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Design</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Anterior nasal swabs were collected from individuals for a nucleic acid amplification test (NAAT) for detection of SARS-CoV-2. A subset of NAAT positive samples was sequenced to determine variants associated with infections. Included in the testing and sequencing protocol were individuals that were fully vaccinated.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Setting</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>This study was performed as part of a surveillance program for SARS-CoV-2 on a university campus with 49,700 students and employees.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Participants</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Surveillance testing was random and included approximately 10% of the population each week. Additionally, individuals self-identified with COVID-19 related symptoms or those that had close contact with SARS-CoV-2 positive individuals were also tested.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: SARS-CoV-2 Breakthrough Infections in Fully Vaccinated Individuals

reference.unstructured: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention. COVID Data Tracker. https://covid.cdc.gov/covid-data-tracker/#datatracker-home. Accessed May 17, 2021.
